history: Jeff has a history of working in environments characterized by micromanagement, which has reinforced his tendency to internalize blame. He reports a recurring theme of self-blame in professional contexts, often thinking 'it's my fault' when workplace issues arise. He describes feeling 'trapped' and 'constantly watched' at work, echoing similar experiences in past jobs. Jeff notes feeling 'isolated' and 'unsupported' by colleagues, and he tends to withdraw from interactions to avoid criticism.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I make a mistake or don't meet expectations, others will see me as incompetent and I will be blamed. I must avoid drawing attention to myself to prevent criticism. If I am constantly monitored, it means I am failing and not living up to what is expected of me.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I keep being micromanaged and blamed, it proves that I will never be good enough and nothing I do will make a difference. No matter how hard I try, I will always fall short and feel trapped in this cycle.
coping_strategies: Jeff withdraws from colleagues and avoids interactions that might lead to criticism, keeping his head down to get through the day. He engages in rumination, replaying work interactions and fixating on perceived failures. He seeks reassurance from his partner, but finds this only provides temporary relief.
situation: Experiencing micromanagement at the workplace and considering resigning.
auto_thought: I'm being watched because I'm not good enough. No matter what I do, they'll find something wrong and I'll end up having to leave.
behavior: I withdraw from colleagues and avoid speaking up in meetings. I keep my head down and try to get through the day without drawing attention. I ruminate about work interactions and seek reassurance from my partner.
